author: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
In which Augustin replies to all the several statements in the letter of Petilianus, as though disputing with an adversary face to face.

title: Chapter 39

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Chapter 39.—92.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Petilianus said: "But there is no fellowship of darkness with light, nor any fellowship of bitterness with the sweet of honey; there is no fellowship of life with death, of innocence with guilt, of water with blood; the lees have no fellowship with oil though they are related to it as being its dregs, but everything that is reprobate will flow away.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: It is the very sink of iniquity; according to the saying of John, ‘They went out from us, but they were not of us; for if they had been of us, they would no doubt have continued with us.’

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: There is no gold among their pollution: all that is precious has been purged away.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For it is written, ‘As gold is tried in the furnace, so also are the just tried by the harassing of tribulation.’

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Cruelty is not a part of gentleness, nor religion a part of sacrilege; nor can the party of Macarius in any way be part of us, because he pollutes the likeness of our rite.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For the enemy’s line, which fills up an enemy’s name, is no part of the force to which it is opposed; but if it is truly to be called a part, it will find a suitable motto in the judgment of Solomon, ‘Let their part be cut off from the earth.’"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Augustin answered: What is it but sheer madness to utter these taunts without proving anything?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: You look at the tares throughout the world, and pay no heed to the wheat, although both have been bidden to grow together throughout the whole of it.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: You look at the seed sown by the wicked one, which shall be separated in the time of harvest, and you pay no heed to the seed of Abraham, in which all nations of the earth shall be blessed.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Just as though you were already a purged mass, and virgin honey, and refined oil, and pure gold, or rather the very similitude of a whited wall.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For, to say nothing of your other faults, do the drunken form a portion of the sober, or are the covetous reckoned among the portion of the wise?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If men of gentle temper appropriate the term of light, where shall the madness of the Circumcelliones be esteemed to be, excepting in the darkness?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Why then is baptism, given by men like these, held valid among you, and the same baptism of Christ not held valid, by whatsoever men it may be administered throughout the world?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: You see, in fact, that you are separated from the communion of the whole world in so far as this, that you are not indeed all drunk, nor all of you covetous, nor all men of violence, but that you are all heretics, and, in virtue of this, are all impious and all sacrilegious.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But as to your saying that the whole world that rejoices in Christian communion is the party of Macarius, who with any remnant of sanity in his brain could make such a statement?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But because we say that you are of the party of Donatus, you therefore seek for a man of whose party you may say we are; and, being in a great strait, you mention the name of some obscure person, who, if he is known in Africa, is certainly unknown in any other quarter of the globe.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And therefore hearken to the answer made to you by all the seed of Abraham from every corner of the earth: Of that Macarius, to whose party you assert us to belong, we know absolutely nothing.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Can you reply in turn that you know nothing of Donatus?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But even if we were to say that you are the party of Optatus, which of you can say that he is unacquainted with Optatus, unless in the sense that he does not know him personally, as perhaps he does not know Donatus either?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But you acknowledge that you rejoice in the name of Donatus, do you also take any pleasure in the name of Optatus?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: What then can the name of Donatus profit you, when all of you alike are polluted by Optatus?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: What advantage can you derive from the sobriety of Donatus, when you are defiled by the drunkenness of the Circumcelliones?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: What, according to your views, are you profited by the innocence of Donatus, when you are stained by the rapacity of Optatus?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For this is your mistake, that you think that the unrighteousness of a man has more power in infecting his neighbor than the righteousness of a man has in purifying those around him.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Therefore, if two share in common the sacraments of God, the one a just man, the other an unrighteous one, but so that neither the former should imitate the unrighteousness of the latter, nor the latter the righteousness of the former, you say that the result is not that both are made just, but that both are made unrighteous; so that also that holy thing, which both receive in common, becomes unclean and loses its original holiness.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: When does unrighteousness find for herself such advocates as these, through whose madness she is esteemed victorious?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: How comes it then that, in the midst of such mistaken perversity, you congratulate yourselves upon the name of Donatus, when it shows not that Petilianus deserves to be what Donatus is, but that Donatus is compelled to be what Optatus is?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But let the house of Israel say, "God is my portion for ever;" let the seed of Abraham say in all nations "The Lord is the portion of mine inheritance."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For they know how to speak through the gospel of the glory of the blessed God.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For you, too, through the sacrament which is in you, like Caiaphas the persecutor of the Lord, prophesy without being aware of it.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For what in Greek is expressed by the word Μαχάριος is in our language simply "Blessed;" and in this way certainly we are of the party of Macarius, the Blessed One.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For what is more blessed than Christ, of whose party we are, after whom all the ends of the earth are called, and to whom they all are turned, and in whose sight all the countries of the nations worship?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Therefore the party of this Macarius, that is to say, of this Blessed One, feels no apprehension at your last curse, distorted from the words of Solomon, lest it should perish from the earth.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For what is said by him of the impious you endeavor to apply to the inheritance of Christ, and you strive to prove that this has been achieved with inexpressible impiety; for when he was speaking of the impious, he says, "Let their portion perish from off the earth."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But when you say, with reference to the words of Scripture, "I shall give Thee the heathen for Thine inheritance," and "all the ends of the world shall remember and turn unto the Lord," that the promise contained in them has already perished from the earth, you are seeking to turn against the inheritance of Christ what was foretold about the lot of the impious; but so long as the inheritance of Christ endures and increases, you are perishing in saying such things.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For you are not in every case prophesying through the sacrament of God, since in this case you are merely uttering evil wishes through your own madness.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But the prophecy of the true prophets is more powerful than the evil speaking of the false prophets.
